topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of damp earth and old bark hit me with a sickening jolt. I stood at the edge of the clearing, my breath catching in my throat. Where the towering oak, the one I’d painstakingly nurtured during those sun-drenched summer afternoons, had stood, there was only a ragged scar on the ground and the skeletal remains of its stump. My hands, usually steady, trembled as I reached out, brushing away fallen leaves that seemed to mock my efforts. *I should have known better. I should have been more attentive. A simple task, and I failed.* A hot flush crept up my neck, and I quickly averted my gaze, focusing instead on the splintered wood, the evidence of my profound inadequacy laid bare.

[story 2]

It was the silence that first alerted me. A profound, unnatural quiet where the rustling symphony of the old tree should have been. I walked toward the spot, my steps heavy and hesitant, each footfall seeming to drag through the disturbed soil. When I finally saw it—the hollowed-out base, the stark, brutal cut—a wave of nausea washed over me. I knelt down, my fingers tracing the rough, freshly exposed wood. A bitter taste filled my mouth. *Look at this mess. Everything I touch seems to crumble or fall apart. I can’t even manage something as simple as planting a sapling to fruition.* I stood abruptly, turning away quickly, desperate to escape the stark reality of what I had allowed to happen.

[story 3]

The sight of the devastation felt like a physical blow. I stared at the cleared patch, my jaw tight, refusing to let my eyes linger too long on the jagged edge of the cut. I remembered the pride I’d felt when I first saw that tiny sprout, the belief that I possessed some innate ability to coax life from the dirt. Now, that belief felt flimsy, a cruel mirage. A low, ragged sound escaped my chest, a sound of deep disappointment. "Unbelievable," I muttered to the empty air, my voice tight with a frustration that coiled in my stomach. I clenched my fists until my knuckles turned white, wanting to punch the very ground that had betrayed me.
